posts.title: Lalique Archers Vase
posts.text:
Twelve archers in a circle, all drawing their bows outward. René Lalique pressed them into green-gray glass in 1921, each figure barely two inches tall but caught mid-pull with the string taut.

The glass is cased — a layer of clear over colored — then acid-etched to pull the figures out of the surface. You don't see them at first. The archers emerge as the light shifts.

Lalique made this at Wingen-sur-Moder, the factory he built in Alsace after outgrowing his Paris workshop. By 1921 he'd abandoned jewelry entirely. Glass was the thing now.

René Lalique, Archers Vase, 1921. Art Institute of Chicago.

posts.title: Skyscraper Cabinet
posts.text:
Paul Frankl looked at the Manhattan skyline and built a bookcase.

His "Skyscraper" furniture line, started around 1926, stacked geometric blocks into setback silhouettes that mirrored the new zoning law towers going up all over Midtown. This cabinet is painted wood — no expensive veneers, no gilt hardware. The geometry does the work.

Frankl was born in Vienna, trained under Josef Hoffmann, arrived in New York in 1914 and never went back. He opened a gallery on 48th Street selling what he called "American modernism" to anyone who'd listen.

Most of these cabinets were made to order. No factory line. Each one slightly different.

Paul Theodore Frankl, Skyscraper Cabinet, c. 1927. Art Institute of Chicago.

posts.title: Cobra Lamp
posts.text:
A cobra made of bronze, hood flared, holding a glass shade in its mouth. Edgar Brandt, 1925.

Brandt was France's most sought-after ironworker. His shop on Boulevard Murat turned out everything from elevator doors to radiator covers, but the animal lamps were what people wanted for their apartments. The cobra. The pelican. The parrot.

The glass shade is attributed to Daum — the Nancy glassworks that supplied half the lampmakers in Paris. Brandt bent the metal. Daum blew the glass. Together they cost more than most people's furniture.

Brandt showed the cobra at the 1925 Paris Exposition — the same fair that gave Art Deco its name.

Edgar Brandt, Cobra Lamp, c. 1925. Art Institute of Chicago.

posts.title: Manhattan Cocktail Set
posts.text:
Norman Bel Geddes designed a cocktail set that looks like it landed from the future.

Chrome-plated brass, 1934. The shaker is a skyscraper in miniature — stepped shoulders tapering to a finial. The tray is a perfect circle. Six cups nest into it like engine cylinders. Everything fits. Nothing extra.

Bel Geddes wasn't a metalworker. He was a theater set designer who wandered into industrial design and never left. He designed the Futurama exhibit at the 1939 World's Fair. He redesigned stove ranges for Standard Gas. He proposed a teardrop car, a flying wing, a floating airport.

The cocktail set was one of the few things that actually got manufactured. Revere Copper and Brass produced it. Not many survive.

Norman Bel Geddes, Manhattan Cocktail Set, 1934-35. Art Institute of Chicago.

posts.title: Georg Jensen Cocktail Shaker
posts.text:
Georg Jensen, 1926. Solid silver. Copenhagen.

No ornament. No engraving. Just the shape — a gentle taper, a domed lid, and a pour spout that curves like it was drawn in a single breath. The handle is ebony.

Jensen started as a sculptor. Switched to silver in 1904 because sculptors starved and silversmiths didn't. By the 1920s his Copenhagen workshop employed 250 people and supplied half the embassies in Europe.

This shaker predates the American cocktail craze by about three years. Jensen was making barware for European diplomats who drank martinis before Manhattan did.

Georg Jensen, Cocktail Shaker, 1926. Art Institute of Chicago.

posts.title: Ceres
posts.text:
John Storrs made a 30-foot version of this for the top of the Chicago Board of Trade Building. This is the maquette — two feet tall, copper alloy plated first with nickel, then chrome.

Ceres. Roman goddess of grain. She stands at the top of the tallest building in Chicago (in 1930), holding a sheaf of wheat in her left hand. Below her, men in suits traded the wheat futures that built the city.

Storrs studied under Rodin in Paris. Came back to America and made sculpture that looked like buildings. Angular. Stepped. Metallic. The art world mostly ignored him. The architects loved him.

He died in 1956. The retrospective didn't happen until 1987.

John Bradley Storrs, Ceres, 1928. Art Institute of Chicago.

posts.title: Diana the Huntress Wall Panel
posts.text:
Screen-printed linen, 1925. Diana with a bow, two greyhounds at her feet, a forest of stylized trees behind her. André Marty designed it. Maison Isidore Leroy printed it in Mulhouse.

Six feet tall, meant to dominate a room. Hang it flat like a tapestry or stretch it across a folding screen.

Marty was an illustrator first — he drew for Gazette du Bon Ton, the fashion magazine that defined Parisian taste before Vogue existed. His textile designs have the same flat perspective and clean outlines.

The greyhounds are pure Deco shorthand. Speed. Luxury. Sleekness. Every Deco designer used them.

André Marty, "Diane Chasseresse", 1925. Art Institute of Chicago.

posts.title: Puiforcat Tea and Coffee Service
posts.text:
Silver, lapis lazuli, ivory, and gold. Jean Puiforcat, Paris, around 1922.

Puiforcat was 25 when he made this. Already the best silversmith in France and he knew it. While everyone else was still doing flowers and scrollwork, he stripped everything back to geometry. Cylinders. Spheres. Flat planes of polished silver interrupted by bands of blue stone.

The lapis lazuli isn't decoration. It's insulation. The blue stone handles keep your fingers off hot metal. Form following function before anyone in Paris was using that phrase.

He produced about 3,000 designs in his career. Died at 48 in 1945. Most of his work is now in private collections where nobody sees it.

Jean E. Puiforcat, Tea and Coffee Service, ca. 1922. Metropolitan Museum of Art.

posts.title: Fantasque Vase by Clarice Cliff
posts.text:
Clarice Cliff, "Fantasque" vase. Earthenware. Stoke-on-Trent, sometime between 1929 and 1939.

Cliff was a factory painter at A.J. Wilkinson Ltd. She started at 13, painting freehand borders on cheap tableware. By 27 she was running her own studio inside the factory, hand-painting what she wanted on shapes she designed herself.

The Fantasque line was her breakout. Bold, abstract landscapes in orange, blue, green, and black. No subtlety. Every piece screamed from the shelf. Department stores couldn't keep them in stock.

She employed about 70 women to paint her designs — the "Bizarre Girls." Each piece was hand-painted, so no two are exactly alike. Collectors now pay thousands for pieces that originally sold for shillings.

Clarice Cliff, "Fantasque" Vase, 1929-39. Metropolitan Museum of Art.

Clarice Cliff, "Fantasque" Vase, 1929-39. Met Museum.

posts.title: Chanel Coat
posts.text:
Silk and metal. House of Chanel, around 1927.

Chanel was 44. She'd already killed the corset, introduced jersey knit to high fashion, and launched No. 5. This coat is from her peak period — simple cut, luxe materials, zero ornament except the fabric itself.

The metal thread woven through the silk catches light as the wearer moves. It's not sequins or beading. The sparkle is structural. Built into the cloth.

By 1927 Chanel employed 2,600 women and dressed half the women who mattered in Paris, London, and New York. Her competitors were still doing elaborate embroidery. She was doing this.

Gabrielle Chanel, Coat, ca. 1927. Metropolitan Museum of Art, Costume Institute.

posts.title: Art Deco Trumpet in B-flat
posts.text:
A trumpet. Brass, silver plate, mother-of-pearl. Made by Anton Riedl in Czechoslovakia, around 1930.

Art Deco hit everything. Furniture, buildings, jewelry, posters. Also musical instruments. This trumpet has faceted bell sections and geometric engraving that would look right on the Chrysler Building elevator doors.

The mother-of-pearl finger buttons are a nice touch. Most trumpets had plain nickel caps. Riedl made them decorative because someone was going to look at this thing on a bandstand and he wanted it to look like it belonged in 1930.

Not much is known about Riedl. Czech instrument maker, active in the interwar period. This is one of the only pieces of his in a major museum collection.

Anton Riedl, Trumpet in B-flat, ca. 1930. Metropolitan Museum of Art.

posts.title: Forms in Space No. 1
posts.text:
Copper and nickel silver, about two feet tall. John Storrs, around 1927.

This is a sculpture that thinks it's a skyscraper. Or a skyscraper that thinks it's a sculpture. Storrs never decided and didn't care. He stacked geometric volumes — rectangles, setbacks, tapered shafts — in the same proportions architects were using for the new towers going up in Chicago and New York.

Storrs showed this kind of work at the 1927 Arts Club of Chicago exhibition. The critics didn't know what to do with it. Was it architecture? Abstract art? Industrial design? All three.

He split his time between Chicago and a castle he bought in the Loire Valley. The French loved him. America forgot about him. His studio was looted during WWII and he spent three months in an internment camp.

John Storrs, Forms in Space No. 1, ca. 1927. Metropolitan Museum of Art.
